title: Cyberpunk 2077
game_description: Cyberpunk 2077 is an open-world, action-adventure RPG set in the dark future of Night City — a dangerous megalopolis obsessed with power, glamor, and ceaseless body modification.
review_text: Cyberpunk 2077 is probably one of my all-time favorite games. I 100% completed it on Xbox, and when it released on Mac, I bought it again and did another full playthrough. Across both platforms, I’ve spent around 500 hours in the game. If you start breaking it down, there’s something to nitpick in almost every part of it. But CD Projekt RED has this kind of magic that makes the whole world just work, even if individual pieces aren’t perfect. Despite its flaws, the game offers one of the most beautiful worlds I’ve ever seen. The art, combat (both melee and gunplay), music, sound design, quests, story, characters, and DLC — all of it came together to create one of the most memorable gaming experiences I’ve ever had. I often get tired of games in the second half. Sometimes I drop them, sometimes I push through. But Cyberpunk was different. I actually delayed finishing it as much as I could, just to stay in this world a little longer. A timeless masterpiece.

Literally just have the npcs grab you and do it deliberately at least, rather then have tech/int characters talked into comprising their security.

title: Cyberpunk 2077
game_description: Cyberpunk 2077 is an open-world, action-adventure RPG set in the dark future of Night City — a dangerous megalopolis obsessed with power, glamor, and ceaseless body modification.
review_text: I bought this game on the Jan 5 2026 and bought Phantom Liberty a short while later because the game was fun. I have 45 out of 57 of the achievements. Initially I bought the game because of Edgerunners and Keanu Reeves… This isn’t the type of game I would normally play. I’ve completed multiple endings and had a lot of fun. Despite having an average spec non gaming PC the game runs smoothly and has not crashed, I feel the initial problems are long gone! I don’t usually play shooters, but the story has been extremely good and thought provoking, particularly in our times. I feel Keanu was pivotal to the success of the story, and who wouldn’t want to ride on a roller-coaster with Johnny! However, I think its also important to acknowledge that the story telling nature of the game, is also the only limitation I would mention. The story means that some events feel highly controlled and there are limited options in order to tell the story. Occasionality I was left feeling I wanted to do something else instead…. I hope it will be possible to find a way for Keanu to return in the sequel.

title: ELDEN RING
game_description: THE CRITICALLY ACCLAIMED FANTASY ACTION RPG. Rise, Tarnished, and be guided by grace to brandish the power of the Elden Ring and become an Elden Lord in the Lands Between.
review_text: My journey into Elden Ring began with confidence. I stepped into this majestic, mysterious world… and immediately locked eyes with a gigantic knight on a horse. “Ah,” I thought. “You are mine.” Reader, I was not his. I charged in at level 1 like a hero in a prophecy. The prophecy was written by a liar. I died. Then I died again. And again. After ten attempts, I began to suspect that perhaps the game and I had… different expectations. But here’s the trick: the world doesn’t shove you down a hallway. It whispers. Golden lights guide you forward, fires let you rest, and somewhere out there is your destiny… probably holding a weapon the size of a small building. And scattered across this hauntingly beautiful world are messages from other players. Helpful guidance, you think. “Treasure ahead.” You follow it. It’s a bloodstained cliff. Another message: “Try finger, but hole.” No further explanation. Just vibes. The gameplay loop is simple: Fight. Roll. Die. Fight better. Roll faster. Die with slightly more dignity. Then suddenly… you don’t die. You win. And when you level up enough, you return to that smug, oversized boss that bullied you earlier… and absolutely erase it from existence. Sweet, vengeful poetry. Or, if you’re feeling less heroic, you summon help. And that help is almost always a nearly naked warrior with a pot on their head and two swords, who arrives like a feral tornado and deletes the boss while you’re still figuring out which button is “heal.” Visually? Stunning. Every landscape looks like it was painted by a melancholic god. Mechanically? Brutal. Your keyboard and mouse will develop trust issues. Emotionally? YOU DIED. After roughly 10,000 deaths, 4 playthroughs, multiple dragon-related incidents, and being humbled by creatures that might actually be gods… I highly recommend it.

title: Red Dead Redemption 2
game_description: Arthur Morgan and the Van der Linde Gang are outlaws on the run. With federal agents and bounty hunters massing on their heels, the gang must rob, steal, and fight their way across the rugged heartland in order to survive.
review_text: I’m not a huge fan of Westerns or that whole genre And this game didn’t change that. That said, I have to admit that this game is a top-tier title, both in terms of gameplay and story. But let’s start from the beginning. In Red Dead Redemption II, we play as Arthur Morgan, a member of a gang or group of outlaws who, under the leadership of Dutch Van Der Linde, travel across the United States to earn money to escape the country. This money, of course, is to be obtained through not-so-legal ventures, so it comes with significant risk. Let’s talk for a moment about the gameplay itself. At first, exploring the map seemed boring and tedious to me; riding a horse for what felt like long distances even started to annoy me at one point. However, the more time I spent in the game, the more joy I derived from such carefree riding without any specific goal. And while the world map isn’t filled with a ton of markers, something is happening every few moments. The world is simply alive; we encounter various animals and people along the way who need our help or want to rob us. There’s actually a lot going on, but it’s not overly intrusive. Graphics are something I usually don't pay much attention to when it comes to video games. RDR2, however, shows us that it's possible to create a beautiful, enjoyable game with a lot of detail. Animations that are simply overlooked in most games, where developers decide there’s no point in creating extra animations because who cares. Here, such little details can be a treat for the eyes. After all, the developers didn’t have to add skinning animations; it would have been enough to include two models—one with skin and one without—but here we see our character cutting the animal’s hide. It’s seemingly nothing important, but it does the job and enhances immersion. The soundtrack is also good; it adapts well to the situation, whether we’re calmly riding down a path or being chased by a group of bandits. There are also plenty of ambient sounds—I’m talking about the various sounds you might hear in the forest, as well as animals and so on. Plus, the voice actors did a great job in their roles. There’s also an online mode, which has its own short storyline and is mostly based on grinding specific roles, such as collecting items or hunting outlaws. It’s actually pretty interesting at first, but over time it gets very monotonous and tedious. The mode is more of a curiosity and not really worth recommending. Summary RDR2 is one of the best video games ever released; there’s no debate about that, and it’s one of those games that simply everyone should play. Even if, like me, you don’t like this kind of atmosphere or this era in world history, it’s worth pushing through the prologue and giving this game a chance—I guarantee you won’t be disappointed. ✔️Great storyline and well-written characters ✔️The graphics in the game are simply insane ✔️The game world is constantly alive, especially the animals ✔️Great voice acting ✔️Lots of options when it comes to interacting with NPCs Time of completion: around 60 hours (single-player mode with relaxed gameplay) Rating: 10/10 ●▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ஜ۩۞۩ஜ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬● Follow my curator for more reviews ●▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ஜ۩۞۩ஜ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬● ATTENTION! summary: I’m not a huge fan of Westerns or that whole genre And this game didn’t change that. That said, I have to admit that this game is a top-tier title, both in terms of gameplay and story. But let’s start from the beginning. In Red Dead Redemption II, we play as Arthur Morgan, a member of a gang or group of outlaws who, under the leadership of Dutch Van Der Linde, travel across the United States to earn money to escape the country. This money, of course, is to be obtained through not-so-legal ventures, so it comes with significant risk. Let’s talk for a moment about the gameplay itself. At first, exploring the map seemed boring and tedious to me; riding a horse for what felt like long distances even started to annoy me at one point. However, the more time I spent in the game, the more joy I derived from such carefree riding without any specific goal. And while the world map isn’t filled with a ton of markers, something is happening every few moments. The world is simply alive; we encounter various animals and people along the way who need our help or want to rob us. There’s actually a lot going on, but it’s not overly intrusive. Graphics are something I usually don't pay much attention to when it comes to video games. RDR2, however, shows us that it's possible to create a beautiful, enjoyable game with a lot of detail. Animations that are simply overlooked in most games, where developers decide there’s no point in creating extra animations because who cares. Here, such little details can be a treat for the eyes. After all, the developers didn’t have to add skinning animations; it would have been enough to include two models—one with skin and one without—but here we see our character cutting the animal’s hide. It’s seemingly nothing important, but it does the job and enhances immersion. The soundtrack is also good; it adapts well to the situation, whether we’re calmly riding down a path or being chased by a group of bandits. There are also plenty of ambient sounds—I’m talking about the various sounds you might hear in the forest, as well as animals and so on. Plus, the voice actors did a great job in their roles. There’s also an online mode, which has its own short storyline and is mostly based on grinding specific roles, such as collecting items or hunting outlaws. It’s actually pretty interesting at first, but over time it gets very monotonous and tedious. The mode is more of a curiosity and not really worth recommending. Summary RDR2 is one of the best video games ever released; there’s no debate about that, and it’s one of those games that simply everyone should play. Even if, like me, you don’t like this kind of atmosphere or this era in world history, it’s worth pushing through the prologue and giving this game a chance—I guarantee you won’t be disappointed. ✔️Great storyline and well-written characters ✔️The graphics in the game are simply insane ✔️The game world is constantly alive, especially the animals ✔️Great voice acting ✔️Lots of options when it comes to interacting with NPCs Time of completion: around 60 hours (single-player mode with relaxed gameplay) Rating: 10/10 ●▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ஜ۩۞۩ஜ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬● Follow my curator for more reviews ●▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ஜ۩۞۩ஜ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬● ATTENTION! review_text: I loaded up Crimson Desert thinking I’d follow the story, rebuild my mercenary crew, and maybe save the world or whatever. Anyway… 16 hours later I own three horses, accidentally adopted a bird, started a campfire cooking career, and I’m currently wanted by at least two factions because I “liberated” their stuff. The game introduces you as this serious mercenary trying to survive in a brutal world… and then immediately lets you ignore all of that to go fishing, climb a mountain, or fistfight a guy for his trousers. The story is technically there, but it’s more like a suggestion than a priority. At one point I set out to do a main quest. On the way, I: • rescued someone’s sheep • got distracted by a random cave • fought a boss I definitely wasn’t ready for • glided off a cliff like I was in the wrong game • forgot what I was doing entirely The combat feels like someone mashed together sword fighting, wrestling, and pure chaos, and somehow it works. You can punch, grapple, swing, and improvise like a medieval action movie that forgot to hire a stunt coordinator. Also, the world is ridiculously big. Not “big for marketing” big, actually get lost for hours and question your life choices big. Is the story amazing? Not really. Is anything explained properly? Also no. But will you randomly spend 40 minutes chasing a side activity you didn’t plan to do? Absolutely. Final verdict: It’s like Skyrim had a chaotic cousin who lifts weights, ignores the main plot, and keeps dragging you into side quests you didn’t agree to. 10/10 would forget my main objective again. P.S: I have an army of cats now, still dot know where Im at the main quest...

review_text: This is a game that's half as good as it is clever, the graphics are amazing, the gameplay is great, the story is decent enough. BUT, there's just too many rough edges. Climbing is hit and miss. Inventory management is a chore. There's just dozens of nested menus for everything. Someimtes the character stops attacking during combat for no reason. A lot of puzzles are so convoluted that a walkthrough is required just to avoid walking around for 2 hours looking for 5 specific trees. This was close to becoming the next skyrim, but it's too similar to othe rmodern games, too many devs working on too many systems and each of them is just a little bit broken, and with just enough friction to stop it becoming one of the greats. If you love open world games like skyrim or zelda, get it. Otherwise it won't be for you.

review_text: It's a good and casual ARPG to sink a bunch of hours into it, especially if you can get it on a 50% or more discount. The optimization is good, which says a lot, and, I had fun doing sidequests & dungeons. The story is, well, manageable, not really good, but you can survive it. The monetization, however, is awful. And what they did to previous DLC owners when releasing Lord of Hatred is pretty damn depressing. For context if you bought the 1st DLC (Vesssel of Hatred) you don't even get a discount for the bundle that includes the old one & new one (Lord of Hatred), pretty sad.

title: Crusader Kings III
game_description: Love, fight, scheme, and claim greatness. Determine your noble house’s legacy in the sprawling grand strategy of Crusader Kings III. Death is only the beginning as you guide your dynasty’s bloodline in the rich and larger-than-life simulation of the Middle Ages.
review_text: Vast improvements on CK2 in many aspects, in particular when it comes to customizing your character/realm/culture/religion (which are key aspects of this series to me). Generally CK3 offers a ton more options on things to do and deviates from the linearity of its predecessor. Unfortunately the game is already suffering from DLC bloat, something that is not out of the ordinary for the Paradox experience. Modding-wise: The future looks very promising for the modding scenario of CK3. In some aspects it has vastly surpassed CK2, but in others there is still a lot of progress to be made.

title: Firestone – Idle Clicker Online RPG
game_description: Chill out, your heroes got this! They’ll fight even when you’re offline. Pop back in for satisfying numbers going up, sweet rewards and easy level-ups. Unlock cool gear, & smack down epic bosses. Play idle or clicker - you’ll power up with no stress, just good times and infinite progression!
review_text: I’ve put close to 1000 hours into Firestone – Idle Clicker Online RPG, and honestly, that’s the only reason I feel qualified to say this: I can’t recommend it. At its core, the game is actually fun. The idle progression, hero collection, and steady upgrades are satisfying, and it’s easy to see why it hooks people. It’s designed to be a chill, AFK-style experience where your team progresses even when you’re offline . But the deeper you go, the more the monetization completely undermines everything good about it. This game is massively pay-to-win. You can technically play for free, but reaching anything close to the top without spending money is basically impossible. Progress slows to a crawl, and you’re constantly hit with FOMO-driven offers, limited-time deals, and “special” bundles clearly designed to push you into spending. It stops feeling like a game and starts feeling like a storefront. Another major issue is how the servers are handled. New servers keep popping up, drawing in fresh players, while older servers stagnate and eventually get merged together. Meanwhile, the real activity and competition stay on the newest servers, making long-term investment feel pointless. So yeah—fun gameplay loop, but buried under predatory P2W systems, FOMO tactics, and questionable server management. Even after 1000 hours, I can’t recommend it.

Overall would recommend, however FIX YOUR OPTIMIZATION KURO THE LATER AREAS OF THE MAP MAKE THE GAME DROP BELOW 30 FKIN FPS!!!!, AND ALSO THE GAME IS 150 GB

title: FINAL FANTASY XIV Online

review_text: A genuine masterpiece of an MMO, with the story telling and world building of a single player RPG. With heart touching music. I cannot count how many times this game has stunned me, awed me, made me despair, made me cry, and gave me hope. The community is the friendliest place on the internet I've ever seen. They will not curse you, or leave you behind when you begin to fall back. Instead, they will pick you up, show and teach you the ways. They will support you with advice, praise, and sometimes gifts. All the memories I've made in this game may not be real life experiences, but they are memories I will hold dear nonetheless. It has a slow burn beginning that might be a drag at first, but when you get to the 1st expansion and begin experiencing the story it will all be sooooo worth it. By the time you get to Endwalker you will be in tears in almost every cutscene. I have not made it to Dawntrail so i cannot comment on that expansion, so I leave my review to end here. But, may you go with the blessing of light, my friends.

title: FINAL FANTASY XIV Online

review_text: You'll experience the best story you've ever played in any videogame though it starts slow af. Create a character and play any class and crafter on it just by switching the weapon - no need for alts. Combat system is tab target and also starts slow af - GCD is as long as the last minute on the display of your washing machine. But it get's better and better while you level up and collect more skills. Rest is like most other MMOs - especially like WoW: Raids, Dungeons, Achievements, Collecting mounts and pets and getting better gear. If you're into MMORPGs like WoW you'll get one of the best games there ever was.
